title: BRRRR Calculator: How to Analyze a BRRRR Deal Step by Step
excerpt: The BRRRR strategy can build serious wealth, but only if the numbers work. Learn how to use a BRRRR calculator to analyze every deal before you commit a single dollar.
author: AIWholesail Team
tags: how to analyze a BRRRR deal
metaDescription: Learn how to analyze a BRRRR deal using a BRRRR calculator. Step-by-step guide with real numbers, examples, and free tools for real estate investors.
metaKeywords: BRRRR calculator, how to analyze a BRRRR deal, BRRRR strategy, BRRRR real estate investing, buy rehab rent refinance repeat, BRRRR deal analysis, rental property calculator, cash-out refinance real estate

sections.content: The BRRRR method — Buy, Rehab, Rent, Refinance, Repeat — is one of the most powerful wealth-building strategies in real estate. Done right, you can recycle the same capital over and over to build a rental portfolio without constantly injecting new money. But done wrong, you can get stuck holding a property that won't refinance, won't rent, or won't cash flow. That's why knowing how to use a BRRRR calculator to analyze a BRRRR deal is not optional — it's essential. If you're new to deal analysis in general, start with our [How to Analyze Real Estate Deals Like a Pro](https://aiwholesail.com/guides/real-estate-deal-analysis) guide before diving in here.
What Is the BRRRR Strategy?

BRRRR stands for Buy, Rehab, Rent, Refinance, Repeat. You purchase a distressed property below market value, renovate it to increase its value, rent it out to generate income, then do a cash-out refinance based on the new appraised value. The goal is to pull out most or all of your original investment so you can use that capital to buy the next deal. In strong rental markets like [Atlanta](https://aiwholesail.com/markets/atlanta-ga), [Dallas](https://aiwholesail.com/markets/dallas-tx), and [Phoenix](https://aiwholesail.com/markets/phoenix-az), savvy investors are running this cycle multiple times a year.
The 6 Numbers Every BRRRR Calculator Must Include

A proper BRRRR deal analysis is more complex than a simple fix-and-flip or wholesale calculation. You're not just looking at purchase price and rehab costs — you need to model rental income, refinance potential, and long-term cash flow all at once. Here are the six core inputs any solid BRRRR calculator needs to work with.

items: Purchase Price: What you pay to acquire the property, ideally well below market value
Rehab Costs: The total cost to renovate the property to rent-ready condition
After Repair Value (ARV): The estimated market value after renovations are complete
Monthly Rent: The gross rent you expect to collect from tenants
Refinance Loan-to-Value (LTV): Most lenders offer 70-75% LTV on cash-out refis for investment properties
Operating Expenses: Property taxes, insurance, property management, maintenance, and vacancy

sections.content: Use our free [BRRRR Calculator at aiwholesail.com/tools/brrrr-calculator](https://aiwholesail.com/tools/brrrr-calculator) to plug in your numbers and instantly see your cash left in the deal, monthly cash flow, and cash-on-cash return — no spreadsheet required.
Step-by-Step: How to Analyze a BRRRR Deal
Step 1: Calculate Your Total Investment

Add up every dollar you spend before the refinance. This includes your purchase price, rehab costs, closing costs on the buy side (typically 2-3% of purchase price), holding costs during renovation (loan interest, utilities, insurance), and any financing fees if you're using hard money. Let's use a real example. Say you buy a property for $110,000, spend $35,000 on rehab, and have $8,000 in closing and holding costs. Your total cash invested before refinance is $153,000.
Step 2: Determine Your ARV and Refinance Amount

Next, estimate the After Repair Value (ARV) by pulling comparable sales in the neighborhood. If similar renovated homes are selling for $210,000, that's your ARV. At a 75% LTV cash-out refinance, the bank will lend you $157,500. From that, you pay off any existing loan on the property. If you used $153,000 of your own cash with no loan, you'd receive $157,500 back at refinance — getting back more than you put in. That's the power of a well-executed BRRRR. Estimating ARV accurately is critical here; the same principles used in fix-and-flip analysis apply, so check out [Fix and Flip Cost Breakdown: What Investors Get Wrong](https://aiwholesail.com/blog/fix-and-flip-cost-breakdown) to avoid common mistakes.
Step 3: Calculate Cash Left in the Deal

Cash left in the deal (CLID) is the most important BRRRR metric. It tells you how much of your original investment is still tied up in the property after refinancing. Formula: Total Cash Invested minus Refinance Proceeds. In our example: $153,000 minus $157,500 equals negative $4,500. You actually pulled out $4,500 more than you put in — a true infinite return scenario. Most investors aim for less than 20% of their original capital left in the deal. Anything above 30-40% left in starts to defeat the purpose of the strategy.
Step 4: Analyze Monthly Cash Flow

Once you refinance, you now have a long-term rental loan with a monthly payment. You need to make sure the rent covers that payment plus all operating expenses and still generates positive cash flow. Using our example: the $157,500 loan at 7.5% interest over 30 years creates a monthly payment of roughly $1,101. If your property rents for $1,650 per month, you then subtract operating expenses. A standard estimate is 40-50% of gross rent for expenses (taxes, insurance, maintenance, management, vacancy). At 45%, that's $742 in expenses. Your net cash flow is $1,650 minus $1,101 minus $742 equals negative $193. That deal cash flows poorly — time to renegotiate the purchase price or lower your rehab budget.
The BRRRR strategy doesn't make bad deals good — it amplifies whether a deal is good or bad. Run the numbers before you fall in love with the property.
Step 5: Calculate Cash-on-Cash Return

If cash left in the deal is greater than zero, you want to measure your return on that remaining capital. Cash-on-cash return equals annual net cash flow divided by cash left in the deal. If you have $20,000 still in the deal and it cash flows $300 per month, your cash-on-cash return is $3,600 divided by $20,000 equals 18%. That's an excellent return. For a deeper look at how this metric compares to cap rate, read our article on [Cap Rate vs Cash-on-Cash Return: Which Matters More?](https://aiwholesail.com/blog/cap-rate-vs-cash-on-cash)
Common BRRRR Mistakes That Kill Returns

items: Overestimating ARV: If comparables don't support your number, the refinance won't either — always use conservative estimates
Underestimating rehab costs: Scope creep is real; add a 10-15% buffer to your rehab budget every single time
Ignoring seasoning requirements: Many lenders require 6-12 months of ownership before a cash-out refi — factor in those holding costs
Using wrong expense ratios: New investors often underestimate vacancy and maintenance, which destroys projected cash flow
Skipping the cash flow stress test: Model what happens if rates rise 1% or rent drops $100 — if it still works, proceed with confidence

sections.content: Before running a BRRRR analysis, check your deal's wholesale numbers first using the [Wholesale Deal Calculator at aiwholesail.com/tools/wholesale-deal-calculator](https://aiwholesail.com/tools/wholesale-deal-calculator). If the deal doesn't make sense as a wholesale, it probably won't survive the BRRRR math either.
When to Walk Away From a BRRRR Deal

Not every distressed property is a BRRRR candidate. Walk away when the ARV doesn't leave enough room below purchase price and rehab costs to support a 70-75% LTV refinance. Walk away when rent rates in the area won't cover your post-refi mortgage payment and expenses with at least $100-200 per month to spare. And walk away when your total investment exceeds 80% of ARV before you even factor in the refi — that's a recipe for being stuck.
